skill.name: langfuse
skill.description: Guides teams through Langfuse setup for LLM tracing, prompt management, evaluation, datasets, cost tracking, and integrations with OpenAI, LangChain, and LlamaIndex.
skill.summary: Build Langfuse observability workflows for tracing, evaluation, prompt management, and production LLM monitoring.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and unrelated text in SKILL.md. The confirmed risk is medium because the examples send prompts, outputs, user IDs, session IDs, and metadata to Langfuse Cloud or a self-hosted endpoint, which can expose sensitive telemetry if copied without privacy controls.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive LLM Telemetry May Be Sent to External Langfuse Endpoint
medium_findings.description: The examples configure Langfuse with a cloud host and trace user identifiers, session identifiers, prompts, generated outputs, usage data, and metadata. This is a legitimate observability pattern, but it can disclose sensitive user data or prompts if adopted without redaction, consent, retention controls, and endpoint review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly configure a Langfuse host and record user IDs, session IDs, prompts, outputs, usage, and metadata. The pattern is intentional observability rather than malware, so the risk depends on deployment privacy controls.

low_findings.title: Inline Placeholder Secret Pattern in Documentation
low_findings.description: The examples show public_key and secret_key values directly in constructor calls. The values are placeholders, not real credentials, but copied patterns may encourage hardcoded secrets instead of environment-based configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret_key strings are clearly placeholders, so there is no exposed credential. The risk is instructional because users may copy the inline-secret style into application code.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences around Python examples and the related-skills inline code list. No shell execution instruction or executable script was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown delimiters or inline code formatting, not Ruby backtick execution. The file is documentation-only and contains no executable shell wrapper.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Findings Are Not Supported
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert on the description line and the reconnaissance alert on user_id and session_id guidance are unsupported by the file context. No cryptographic algorithm or system discovery behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata and line 225 recommends application identifiers for observability. Neither location contains cryptography or host/system reconnaissance behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Trace Export of Prompts and Model Outputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The code examples demonstrate sending prompt inputs, model outputs, user identifiers, session identifiers, and metadata to Langfuse. This pattern is useful for observability but requires privacy review before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations show a concrete external host and telemetry fields that include user and session identifiers plus prompt and output data. The behavior is legitimate but privacy-sensitive.

content.value_statement: LLM teams need reliable traces, prompt versions, evaluations, and cost signals in production. This skill provides practical Langfuse patterns for instrumenting, reviewing, and improving AI applications.
actual_capabilities: Explains basic Langfuse tracing setup for Python LLM applications.
Shows OpenAI SDK tracing patterns, including streaming and async calls.
Shows LangChain callback handler setup for chains, agents, and retrievers.
Covers trace scoring, user feedback, usage tracking, and metadata capture.
Highlights prompt management, dataset workflows, cost tracking, and evaluation planning.
Identifies operational anti-patterns such as missing flush calls and noisy traces.
limitations: Requires a Langfuse Cloud account or a self-hosted Langfuse deployment.
Does not provide a complete production privacy or compliance policy.
Examples are documentation snippets and may need updates for specific SDK versions.
High-volume tracing may require sampling, batching, and storage planning.

use_cases.title: Instrument a production chatbot
use_cases.description: Add traces, generations, token usage, user feedback, and session grouping to a customer-facing assistant.

use_cases.title: Evaluate prompt changes
use_cases.description: Compare prompt versions with trace scores, datasets, and quality metrics before deploying changes.

use_cases.title: Debug LangChain workflows
use_cases.description: Use Langfuse callback handlers to trace chains, agents, retrievers, latency, and failure points.

prompt_templates.title: Plan my first trace
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add basic Langfuse tracing to a Python OpenAI chat flow. Include trace names, user IDs, usage logging, and flush handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review my instrumentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Langfuse tracing plan for an LLM application. Identify missing spans, metadata, scores, privacy controls, and cost metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Design an evaluation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Langfuse evaluation workflow for prompt version testing. Include datasets, scoring criteria, regression checks, and release gates.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize production observability
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production Langfuse observability strategy for a high-volume LLM service. Cover sampling, retention, redaction, dashboards, and incident review.

output_examples.input: Add observability to a support chatbot.
output_examples.output: A tracing plan with named chat-completion traces, grouped sessions, model usage tracking, helpfulness scores, metadata tags, and a flush strategy.

output_examples.input: Compare two prompt versions before release.
output_examples.output: An evaluation plan using a dataset, quality scores, latency and cost metrics, failure review, and a release recommendation.
A callback-based tracing approach that separates chain steps, agent tool calls, retriever latency, model calls, and final response scoring.

best_practices: Redact sensitive prompts, outputs, identifiers, and metadata before exporting traces.
Use meaningful trace names, session IDs, tags, and scores for reliable debugging.
Flush traces in serverless or short-lived processes so important telemetry is not lost.
anti_patterns: Do not trace every internal operation when a smaller set of spans answers operational questions.
Do not hardcode Langfuse secret keys in application source files or shared notebooks.
Do not rely only on dashboards without datasets, evaluations, and release criteria.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps teams use Langfuse to trace, evaluate, and monitor LLM applications in development and production.

faq.question: Does it support OpenAI applications?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes OpenAI tracing patterns, including standard calls, streaming, and async usage.

faq.question: Can it help with LangChain?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Langfuse callback handler usage for chains, agents, retrievers, and related workflows.

faq.question: Does it require Langfuse Cloud?
faq.answer: No. The examples mention Langfuse Cloud, but Langfuse can also be self-hosted.

faq.question: What privacy controls should I consider?
faq.answer: Review prompt, output, user identifier, session identifier, metadata, retention, access, and redaction policies before production tracing.

faq.question: Can it create a complete monitoring platform?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance, but infrastructure, access control, data retention, and compliance remain your responsibility.
